draft.introduction: Farid ud-Din Attar's Mantiq al-Tayr ("The Conference of the Birds") is the greatest allegorical poem in Sufi literature. It tells of the birds of the world who journey to find the Simurgh, their king, guided by the hoopoe.

translation.text: The hoopoe said: "I am a messenger of the invisible world. I know God and the secrets of creation. When the Beloved shows a single hair from the beauty of His face, the worlds are shattered with longing. All the birds of the world need a king. Our king is the Simurgh. He lives behind Mount Qaf, and the way is long and full of peril. But you must set out, for without the journey, you will remain what you are."

translator_notes.note: The Simurgh (literally "thirty birds" in Persian) is both the goal and the revelation: when the thirty surviving birds finally arrive, they discover that si murgh (thirty birds) is the Simurgh. The self is the divine, seen through the mirror of annihilation. This pun carries the entire metaphysics of the poem.
